era_context: The early-to-mid 2000s were shaped by the aftermath of the 11 September 2001 attacks and the U.S.-led 'War on Terror'. The invasion of Iraq in March 2003 had toppled Saddam Hussein's government, but 2004 saw the country descend into insurgency and sectarian violence. The European Union underwent its largest-ever expansion, absorbing ten new members mostly from post-communist Central and Eastern Europe, reshaping the continent's political geography. China's economic boom continued to accelerate, globalisation deepened, and the seeds of social media and web-based platforms were being planted — Facebook launched in February 2004. Climate change discourse was gaining momentum, and the year ended with one of the deadliest natural disasters in recorded history, the Indian Ocean earthquake and tsunami.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Central African domestic politics beyond the Darfur crisis and Great Lakes conflicts receive comparatively thin coverage
Pacific Island nations outside of tsunami-related reporting are underrepresented
Central Asian politics beyond headline events in Uzbekistan and Kyrgyzstan
Internal dynamics of smaller Southeast Asian states (Laos, Cambodia, Myanmar) outside major international stories

events.title: Mars Exploration Rovers Spirit and Opportunity land on Mars
events.description: NASA's twin Mars Exploration Rovers, Spirit and Opportunity, landed on Mars on 3 January and 25 January respectively. Spirit landed in Gusev Crater and Opportunity in Meridiani Planum. Opportunity quickly discovered geological evidence of past liquid water on Mars — layered sedimentary rocks with sulphate minerals and hematite 'blueberries' — marking a major milestone in planetary science. Both rovers vastly exceeded their planned 90-day missions: Spirit operated until 2010 and Opportunity until 2018, fundamentally reshaping understanding of Martian geology and the planet's potential habitability.

sources.name: Squyres et al., 'In Situ Evidence for an Ancient Aqueous Environment at Meridiani Planum, Mars', Science, Vol. 306, December 2004
NASA Jet Propulsion Laboratory, Mars Exploration Rover Mission press releases and status reports, 2004

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented by NASA with real-time telemetry, imagery, and peer-reviewed publications.

events.title: Facebook founded at Harvard University
events.description: On 4 February 2004, Mark Zuckerberg launched 'TheFacebook' from his Harvard University dormitory room with co-founders Eduardo Saverin, Andrew McCollum, Dustin Moskovitz, and Chris Hughes. Initially restricted to Harvard students, it expanded rapidly to other Ivy League and then broader university campuses throughout 2004. By year's end it had approximately one million registered users. While its long-term significance as one of the most influential technology platforms in history — reshaping social interaction, media, politics, and privacy — was not yet apparent, its rapid uptake signalled the coming dominance of social media platforms.

sources.name: The Harvard Crimson, 'Hundreds Register for New Facebook Website', 9 February 2004
Kirkpatrick, David, 'The Facebook Effect' (2010)

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented by contemporary media and later detailed accounts. Launch date and early growth are well established.

events.title: Madrid train bombings (11-M)
events.description: On 11 March 2004, a series of coordinated bomb attacks on commuter trains at the Atocha, El Pozo, and Santa Eugenia stations in Madrid killed 193 people and wounded approximately 2,000. The bombings, carried out by a jihadist cell inspired by al-Qaeda, were the deadliest terrorist attack in Europe since the 1988 Lockerbie bombing. The attacks occurred three days before Spanish general elections and had immediate political consequences: the ruling Partido Popular under José María Aznar, which initially blamed the Basque separatist group ETA, lost to the Spanish Socialist Workers' Party (PSOE) led by José Luis Rodríguez Zapatero, who subsequently withdrew Spanish troops from Iraq.
key_figures: José María Aznar (outgoing Prime Minister of Spain)
José Luis Rodríguez Zapatero (incoming Prime Minister of Spain)

sources.name: Spanish National Court, indictments and trial proceedings in Audiencia Nacional case 'Sumario 20/2004'
Comisión de Investigación del 11-M, Spanish Congress, final report (2005)
Contemporary reporting by El País, El Mundo, Reuters, and international wire services, March 2004

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented by Spanish judicial proceedings, parliamentary investigation, and international media. Perpetrators identified through trial.

events.title: European Union 'Big Bang' enlargement — ten new member states
events.description: On 1 May 2004, the European Union expanded from 15 to 25 member states in its largest single enlargement. The ten new members were Poland, Czech Republic, Slovakia, Hungary, Slovenia, Estonia, Latvia, Lithuania, Malta, and Cyprus. The accession represented the geopolitical reunification of Europe after the Cold War division, integrating most of the former Eastern Bloc into Western institutional structures. The enlargement reshaped EU internal politics by adding a significant bloc of post-communist states with distinct economic profiles and foreign policy orientations, particularly regarding Russia. It also fuelled migration debates in existing member states, particularly the UK.
key_figures: Romano Prodi (President of the European Commission)

sources.name: Treaty of Accession 2003, signed Athens, 16 April 2003; entered into force 1 May 2004
European Commission, 'Comprehensive Monitoring Reports on Accession Preparations', 2003–2004

events.certainty_note: Treaty-based event with precise legal date of accession. Fully documented.

events.title: Beslan school siege
events.description: On 1 September 2004, Chechen and Ingush militants seized School No. 1 in Beslan, North Ossetia, taking over 1,100 hostages, most of them children and parents attending a first-day-of-school ceremony. The siege lasted three days and ended on 3 September when Russian security forces stormed the building, resulting in the deaths of 334 hostages, including 186 children. The massacre was the deadliest school attack in history and provoked intense domestic and international debate over Russian counterterrorism tactics. President Putin used the crisis to justify significant centralisation of power, including abolishing direct gubernatorial elections in Russian regions.
key_figures: Shamil Basayev (Chechen militant leader who claimed responsibility)

sources.name: North Ossetian Parliamentary Commission report on the Beslan tragedy (2005–2006)
European Court of Human Rights, 'Tagayeva and Others v. Russia' (Application no. 26562/07), judgment 2017
Contemporary reporting by ITAR-TASS, Reuters, BBC, and international wire services, September 2004

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented, though some aspects of the Russian security forces' response remain disputed. The ECHR later found Russia partly responsible for the high death toll.

events.title: Darfur crisis escalates; UN declares humanitarian emergency
events.description: The conflict in Darfur, western Sudan, escalated dramatically in 2004 as government-backed Janjaweed militias continued attacks on non-Arab civilian populations. By mid-2004, the UN described the situation as the world's worst humanitarian crisis, with an estimated 50,000 killed, over one million internally displaced, and hundreds of thousands of refugees in Chad. The U.S. Congress declared the violence genocide in July, and Secretary of State Colin Powell used the term in September testimony, though international response remained limited. The African Union deployed a small monitoring mission (AMIS), which was inadequately resourced. The crisis exposed the limitations of the international community's 'responsibility to protect' doctrine.
key_figures: Jan Pronk (UN Special Representative for Sudan)

sources.name: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs, Darfur situation reports, 2004
U.S. State Department, 'Documenting Atrocities in Darfur', Bureau of Democracy, Human Rights, and Labor, September 2004
International Crisis Group, 'Darfur Rising: Sudan's New Crisis', Africa Report No. 76, March 2004

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented by international organisations, media, and later ICC proceedings. Casualty figures are debated but the scale of atrocity is not disputed.

events.title: Iraqi insurgency intensifies; Abu Ghraib prisoner abuse scandal
events.description: The Iraqi insurgency escalated sharply throughout 2004. Two major battles of Fallujah — an inconclusive assault in April and a massive U.S. offensive in November — became defining engagements of the Iraq War. Simultaneously, in April 2004, photographs emerged showing U.S. military personnel abusing and humiliating Iraqi detainees at Abu Ghraib prison, provoking global outrage and severely damaging American credibility and the moral case for the occupation. The images, broadcast worldwide, became iconic symbols of the war's failure. Insurgent violence, including kidnappings and beheadings of foreign hostages, intensified throughout the year, with the Mahdi Army of Muqtada al-Sadr also fighting coalition forces in Najaf and Sadr City.
key_figures: George W. Bush (President of the United States)
Abu Musab al-Zarqawi (leader of al-Qaeda in Iraq)
Muqtada al-Sadr (Shia cleric and militia leader)
Ayad Allawi (Iraqi Interim Prime Minister from June 2004)

sources.name: Taguba Report (Article 15-6 Investigation of the 800th Military Police Brigade), completed February 2004, leaked April 2004
CBS News 60 Minutes II, initial broadcast of Abu Ghraib photographs, 28 April 2004
International Committee of the Red Cross, report on treatment of prisoners in Iraq, February 2004

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented by U.S. military investigations, photographic evidence, congressional hearings, and international reporting.

events.title: Orange Revolution in Ukraine
events.description: Following the disputed Ukrainian presidential run-off election of 21 November 2004, in which official results declared Viktor Yanukovych the winner over Viktor Yushchenko, massive popular protests erupted in Kyiv and across western and central Ukraine. Demonstrators, identifying with the orange colour of Yushchenko's campaign, occupied the Maidan Nezalezhnosti (Independence Square) for weeks. International observers and exit polls indicated widespread fraud. The Ukrainian Supreme Court annulled the results on 3 December and ordered a revote on 26 December, which Yushchenko won decisively. The Orange Revolution became a symbol of post-Soviet democratic aspiration and deepened geopolitical tensions between Western powers and Russia, which had openly supported Yanukovych.

sources.name: OSCE/ODIHR Election Observation Mission, Ukraine Presidential Election 2004, final report
Ukrainian Supreme Court, decision annulling run-off results, 3 December 2004
Wilson, Andrew, 'Ukraine's Orange Revolution' (Yale University Press, 2005)

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented by international observers, Ukrainian courts, and global media. One of the most thoroughly covered political events of 2004.

events.title: Indian Ocean earthquake and tsunami
events.region: Indian Ocean rim: Indonesia, Sri Lanka, India, Thailand, and ten other countries
events.description: On 26 December 2004, a magnitude 9.1–9.3 undersea earthquake off the west coast of northern Sumatra, the third-largest ever recorded, generated a series of devastating tsunamis that struck the coasts of fourteen countries around the Indian Ocean. The death toll reached approximately 227,000–230,000, making it one of the deadliest natural disasters in recorded history. Indonesia's Aceh province was worst hit with over 160,000 dead, followed by Sri Lanka (approximately 35,000), India (approximately 16,000), and Thailand (approximately 8,000). The disaster exposed the total absence of a tsunami warning system in the Indian Ocean and prompted the largest international humanitarian response in history at that time.

sources.name: U.S. Geological Survey, Earthquake Hazards Program, 'Magnitude 9.1 — Off the West Coast of Northern Sumatra', seismological data and analysis
Tsunami Evaluation Coalition, 'Joint Evaluation of the International Response to the Indian Ocean Tsunami' (2006)
NOAA Pacific Marine Environmental Laboratory, tsunami propagation models and tide gauge data, 2004–2005

events.certainty_note: Among the most extensively documented natural disasters in history through seismological data, satellite imagery, and global media coverage. Total death toll figures vary by source within a range of approximately 220,000–280,000.

events.title: George W. Bush re-elected President of the United States
events.description: On 2 November 2004, President George W. Bush won re-election, defeating Democratic Senator John Kerry of Massachusetts with 50.7% of the popular vote to Kerry's 48.3% and 286 to 251 electoral votes. The election was heavily shaped by the Iraq War, the ongoing 'War on Terror', and so-called 'moral values' issues including same-sex marriage, with ballot initiatives banning it in eleven states thought to have boosted conservative turnout. Ohio proved the decisive state, and though Kerry conceded the following day, concerns about voting irregularities persisted. Bush's re-election ensured continuity of the neoconservative foreign policy agenda and the expansion of executive power in the national security sphere.

sources.name: Federal Election Commission, 'Federal Elections 2004: Election Results for the U.S. President, the U.S. Senate and the U.S. House of Representatives'
National Election Pool exit poll data, 2 November 2004

events.certainty_note: Fully documented U.S. federal election with certified results.

events.title: Indian general election: Congress-led UPA coalition comes to power
events.description: India's general election, held in four phases during April–May 2004, produced a surprise result as the Indian National Congress-led United Progressive Alliance (UPA) defeated the ruling BJP-led National Democratic Alliance under Prime Minister Atal Bihari Vajpayee. The BJP had called early elections expecting to capitalise on strong economic growth under its 'India Shining' campaign, but the results reflected widespread rural discontent and the sense that economic gains had not reached the poor. Manmohan Singh was appointed Prime Minister on 22 May after Congress president Sonia Gandhi, an Italian-born Indian citizen, declined the post amid political controversy. Singh became India's first Sikh Prime Minister.

sources.name: Election Commission of India, General Elections 2004 results and statistical reports
Yadav, Yogendra, 'The Elusive Mandate of 2004', Economic and Political Weekly, Vol. 39, No. 51, December 2004

events.certainty_note: Fully documented national election with certified results from the Election Commission of India.

events.title: NATO admits seven new member states
events.description: On 29 March 2004, Bulgaria, Estonia, Latvia, Lithuania, Romania, Slovakia, and Slovenia formally acceded to NATO, the largest expansion in the alliance's history. The accession of the three Baltic states — formerly part of the Soviet Union itself — was particularly significant geopolitically, bringing NATO's border to within 150 kilometres of St. Petersburg. Russia protested the expansion vigorously. Combined with the EU enlargement of 1 May, the dual accessions represented the most comprehensive institutional integration of the former Eastern Bloc since the Cold War's end and fundamentally redrew Europe's security architecture.
key_figures: Jaap de Hoop Scheffer (NATO Secretary General)
George W. Bush (President of the United States)

sources.name: NATO, 'Accession Protocols to the North Atlantic Treaty', signed 26 March 2003; accession effective 29 March 2004
Asmus, Ronald D., 'Opening NATO's Door' (Columbia University Press, 2002; updated 2004)

events.certainty_note: Treaty-based event with precise legal date. Fully documented.

events.title: Putin re-elected President of Russia
events.description: Vladimir Putin won re-election as President of Russia on 14 March 2004 with 71.3% of the vote, defeating Communist Party candidate Nikolai Kharitonov and several minor candidates in a largely uncompetitive race. The election reflected Putin's consolidated domestic control, boosted by economic growth driven by high oil prices and a degree of stability after the chaos of the 1990s. International observers from the OSCE noted the election fell short of democratic standards due to media bias, lack of genuine political competition, and the use of state resources. Putin's second term would see further centralisation of power, particularly after the Beslan crisis.

sources.name: OSCE/ODIHR, 'Russian Federation Presidential Election, 14 March 2004: Election Observation Mission Final Report'
Central Election Commission of the Russian Federation, official results, March 2004

events.certainty_note: Fully documented election with official certified results and international observer reporting.

events.title: Haitian political crisis: Aristide ousted in coup
events.description: On 29 February 2004, President Jean-Bertrand Aristide departed Haiti amid an armed rebellion and intense international pressure, flying to the Central African Republic on a U.S.-provided aircraft. Aristide, the country's first democratically elected president in 1990 and returned to office in 2000 in a disputed election, maintained he was kidnapped by U.S. forces — a claim denied by Washington. An interim government under Boniface Alexandre and Prime Minister Gérard Latortue was installed. A UN Stabilisation Mission (MINUSTAH) was authorised by the Security Council in June. The crisis highlighted the persistent fragility of Haitian governance and the contested role of foreign powers in Caribbean politics.

sources.name: UN Security Council Resolution 1529 (2004), authorising multinational interim force, 29 February 2004
UN Security Council Resolution 1542 (2004), establishing MINUSTAH, 30 April 2004
International Crisis Group, 'A New Chance for Haiti?', Latin America/Caribbean Report No. 10, November 2004

events.certainty_note: Well documented, though the precise circumstances of Aristide's departure remain contested. Whether he was 'removed' or 'resigned' is a matter of ongoing dispute.

events.title: Athens Olympic Games
events.description: The Games of the XXVIII Olympiad were held in Athens, Greece, from 13 to 29 August 2004, returning the Olympics to the country of their ancient and modern origin. The event involved over 10,600 athletes from 201 countries. Despite chronic construction delays that raised international concerns about readiness, the Games were completed on time and widely regarded as a sporting success, with multiple world records broken. However, the enormous cost — estimated at over €9 billion — contributed to Greece's mounting public debt and became part of the narrative of fiscal imprudence leading to the Greek sovereign debt crisis of 2010. Security costs alone reached an estimated €1.2 billion, reflecting the post-9/11 threat environment.

sources.name: International Olympic Committee, Official Report of the XXVIII Olympiad, Athens 2004
Kasimati, Evangelia, 'Post-Olympic Use of the Olympic Venues: The Case of Greece', Athens University of Economics and Business, Working Papers (2006)

events.certainty_note: Fully documented international event. Cost estimates vary by source but the order of magnitude is consistent.

events.title: SpaceShipOne wins Ansari X Prize: first private crewed spaceflight
events.description: On 4 October 2004, SpaceShipOne completed its second qualifying spaceflight within two weeks, winning the $10 million Ansari X Prize and becoming the first privately funded spacecraft to reach suborbital space twice in 14 days. Designed by Burt Rutan and funded by Microsoft co-founder Paul Allen, the craft was launched from the White Knight carrier aircraft and reached altitudes above 100 km. The achievement demonstrated the viability of private spaceflight and directly inspired the founding of Virgin Galactic, which licensed the technology. It marked the beginning of the commercial spaceflight industry that would grow significantly over the following two decades.

sources.name: X Prize Foundation, official prize announcement and verification, October 2004
Scaled Composites, SpaceShipOne flight logs and press releases, 2004

events.certainty_note: Fully documented with FAA tracking data, independent observers, and media coverage.

events.title: Death of Yasser Arafat
events.description: Yasser Arafat, Chairman of the Palestine Liberation Organisation and President of the Palestinian National Authority, died on 11 November 2004 at the Percy military hospital in Clamart, France, at age 75. He had been confined to his Ramallah compound (the Mukataa) by Israeli forces since 2002. His death removed the dominant figure in Palestinian politics for four decades and opened a succession struggle. Mahmoud Abbas succeeded him as PLO chairman and won the Palestinian presidential election in January 2005. The cause of Arafat's death remained controversial, with subsequent investigations yielding inconclusive results regarding poisoning allegations.
key_figures: Yasser Arafat (PLO Chairman, Palestinian Authority President)

sources.name: Percy military hospital (Hôpital d'Instruction des Armées Percy), medical records (partially released)
Contemporary reporting by Al Jazeera, Reuters, Associated Press, and international wire services, November 2004
Swiss Institute of Radiation Physics, Lausanne, forensic analysis (2012–2013, inconclusive findings on polonium)

events.certainty_note: Death and date are confirmed. Cause of death remains medically debated; poisoning allegations have not been conclusively proven or disproven.

events.title: Google initial public offering (IPO)
events.description: Google Inc. completed its initial public offering on 19 August 2004, using an unconventional Dutch auction process that priced shares at $85, valuing the company at approximately $23 billion. The IPO raised $1.67 billion, making it one of the largest technology IPOs since the dot-com bust. By year's end, the stock had nearly doubled. The IPO was significant not only as a financial milestone but as a marker of the internet economy's recovery from the 2000–2001 crash and the emergence of the 'Web 2.0' era dominated by search, advertising, and data-driven business models. Google's subsequent growth into one of the world's most valuable companies began here.

sources.name: Google Inc., Form S-1 Registration Statement, filed with SEC, 29 April 2004; amended filings through August 2004
U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission, EDGAR filings for Google Inc., 2004

events.certainty_note: Fully documented through SEC filings and market data.

events.title: Enlargement of the Schengen Area delayed; EU Constitutional Treaty signed
events.description: On 29 October 2004, heads of state and government of 25 EU member states and three candidate countries signed the Treaty establishing a Constitution for Europe in Rome. The constitutional treaty aimed to replace the existing EU treaties with a single document streamlining decision-making and creating an EU foreign minister post. However, the treaty ultimately failed when French and Dutch voters rejected it in referendums in May and June 2005, triggering a prolonged institutional crisis. The treaty's ambitions were eventually partially salvaged in the 2007 Treaty of Lisbon, but the referendums exposed a growing gap between EU elites and electorates on integration.
key_figures: Valéry Giscard d'Estaing (President of the European Convention)

sources.name: Treaty establishing a Constitution for Europe, Official Journal of the European Union, C 310, 16 December 2004
European Convention, final draft and documentation, 2003–2004

events.certainty_note: Treaty text and signing ceremony fully documented. Its subsequent rejection is a matter of record.

events.title: Sri Lankan tsunami devastation amid ongoing civil conflict
events.description: Sri Lanka was the second-hardest-hit country by the Indian Ocean tsunami, with approximately 35,000 dead and over 500,000 displaced. The disaster struck a country already in a fragile ceasefire between the government and the Liberation Tigers of Tamil Eelam (LTTE). Initial hopes that the shared catastrophe might catalyse peace were dashed when disputes erupted over the distribution of aid between government-controlled and LTTE-held areas. A proposed joint mechanism for aid distribution (P-TOMS) became a political flashpoint, and the ceasefire gradually deteriorated, eventually collapsing entirely in 2006. The tsunami thus became entangled with the country's ethnic conflict rather than transcending it.

sources.name: Asian Development Bank, 'Sri Lanka: 2005 Post-Tsunami Recovery Program', preliminary damage and needs assessment, 2005
International Crisis Group, 'Sri Lanka: The Failure of the Peace Process', Asia Report No. 124, November 2006

events.certainty_note: Tsunami impact is well documented. The interaction between disaster response and the civil conflict is documented in multiple independent analyses.

events.title: China's economic growth exceeds 10%; 'peaceful rise' doctrine articulated
events.description: China's GDP growth reached 10.1% in 2004, driven by massive fixed-asset investment, export growth, and urbanisation. Concerns about overheating led the government to implement macroeconomic tightening measures. Zheng Bijian, a senior party intellectual, articulated the 'peaceful rise' (heping jueqi) thesis, which was subsequently softened to 'peaceful development' (heping fazhan) by the leadership, framing China's ascent as non-threatening to the existing international order. The concept became central to Chinese foreign policy discourse, though scepticism about its sincerity was widespread internationally. China's accession to the WTO in 2001 was by now transforming global supply chains, and the country became the world's third-largest trading nation.

sources.name: National Bureau of Statistics of China, Statistical Communiqué on National Economic and Social Development, 2004
Zheng Bijian, 'China's Peaceful Rise to Great-Power Status', Foreign Affairs, September/October 2005 (based on 2003–2004 speeches and papers)

events.certainty_note: GDP figures from official Chinese statistics. The 'peaceful rise' conceptualisation is documented in multiple primary and secondary sources.

events.title: Cassini-Huygens spacecraft enters Saturn orbit
events.description: On 1 July 2004, the Cassini-Huygens spacecraft, a joint mission of NASA, the European Space Agency, and the Italian Space Agency, entered orbit around Saturn after a nearly seven-year journey. It became the first spacecraft to orbit Saturn. Initial observations revealed previously unknown details about Saturn's rings, moons, and magnetosphere. On 25 December 2004, the Huygens probe separated from Cassini for its descent to Titan, Saturn's largest moon, landing on 14 January 2005 — the most distant landing ever achieved by a spacecraft. The mission continued until 2017, transforming understanding of the Saturnian system.

sources.name: NASA/JPL, Cassini-Huygens mission status reports and press releases, 2004
Porco, Carolyn et al., 'Cassini Observes the Active South Pole of Enceladus', Science, Vol. 311, March 2006 (early results)

events.certainty_note: Fully documented by mission telemetry, NASA/ESA press communications, and peer-reviewed publications.

events.title: Afghan presidential election: Hamid Karzai elected
events.description: On 9 October 2004, Afghanistan held its first direct presidential election. Hamid Karzai, the interim and transitional leader since the 2001 Bonn Agreement, won with 55.4% of the vote against 22 opponents. Despite significant logistical challenges, security threats, and allegations of fraud — including the use of indelible ink that proved washable — the election was broadly endorsed by international observers as a meaningful step. Approximately 8.1 million Afghans voted, including significant female participation. However, the election took place within a security environment maintained by international forces, and the Taliban, who boycotted and threatened voters, continued to pose a growing insurgent threat.
key_figures: Jean Arnault (UN Special Representative for Afghanistan)

sources.name: Joint Electoral Management Body of Afghanistan, official results certification, November 2004
OSCE/ODIHR, 'Afghanistan Presidential Election, 9 October 2004, Election Support Team Report'

events.certainty_note: Fully documented election with certified results and international observation, though fraud allegations were noted.

disconfirming_evidence: Several commonly cited narratives about 2004 events warrant qualification. (1) The Madrid bombings' direct electoral impact is often presented as a straightforward case of terrorism swaying a democratic election. However, polling data suggests the Partido Popular was already losing ground before 11 March, and scholars argue that the government's attempt to blame ETA rather than jihadists, contradicting emerging evidence, was more electorally damaging than the attack itself. (2) The 'Orange Revolution' is frequently framed as a purely spontaneous popular uprising, but research has documented significant financial and logistical support from Western NGOs, the U.S. government-funded National Endowment for Democracy, and European foundations — complicating the narrative of organic democratic revolution. (3) The Abu Ghraib scandal is sometimes treated as an aberration involving 'a few bad apples,' as characterised by the Bush administration, but the Taguba Report, the Schlesinger Panel, and subsequent investigations documented systemic failures in command, interrogation policy, and oversight that extended well beyond the individual perpetrators prosecuted. (4) Regarding Aristide's departure from Haiti, the characterisation as 'coup' versus 'resignation' remains genuinely contested, with credible testimony on both sides. (5) The death toll of the Indian Ocean tsunami varies significantly across sources — from around 220,000 to over 280,000 — reflecting the difficulty of counting in devastated regions where entire communities were obliterated and many victims were never recovered.
historiographic_note: The year 2004 is extremely well documented through multiple independent source traditions: government records, international organisation reports, extensive global media coverage, satellite imagery, scientific instrumentation, and digital archives. The primary bias in English-language historiography is toward U.S. and European perspectives, particularly regarding the Iraq War and the 'War on Terror.' The Darfur crisis and other African events receive thinner treatment relative to their human impact. Russian domestic coverage of events like Beslan differs substantially from Western reporting, reflecting competing state narratives. Chinese economic data relies significantly on official statistics whose methodology has been questioned by some economists, though the broad trajectory of rapid growth is not disputed. The digital revolution — the emergence of social media, the Google IPO — tends to be narrated through a Silicon Valley lens that obscures global technology developments in Asia and elsewhere. Overall, 2004 benefits from the richest documentation environment in human history up to that point, but that abundance itself creates selection bias: the most-covered events are not necessarily the most consequential.

graph_edges.note: Madrid bombings were part of the broader wave of jihadist attacks following 9/11 and linked to opposition to the Iraq War
EU enlargement was the institutional culmination of the post-Cold War reintegration of Eastern Europe
Iraqi insurgency was a direct consequence of the 2003 invasion and subsequent occupation
The Orange Revolution's incomplete reforms and Yanukovych's later return to power set the stage for the 2014 Euromaidan
The December 2004 tsunami triggered the largest international humanitarian response and the Aceh peace process in 2005
The Beslan siege provided Putin with justification for further centralisation of power already underway after his re-election
NATO and EU enlargements in 2004 were parallel processes integrating the same post-communist states into Western institutions
Athens Olympics cost overruns contributed to Greece's mounting public debt burden
Google's IPO was the financial maturation of the company founded in 1998
Facebook's founding and Google's IPO in the same year marked the emergence of the platform-dominated internet economy
The Darfur atrocities documented in 2004 led to the ICC's 2009 arrest warrant for President al-Bashir
Arafat's political trajectory and the Israeli-Palestinian dynamics at his death were shaped by the Oslo process he co-initiated
The Orange Revolution followed the template of Georgia's Rose Revolution, part of a wave of 'colour revolutions' in the post-Soviet space

_meta.note: Produced via Claude Code CLI (Max subscription) before API migration
